CPSC 582 · Personal Dataset · W2026

9 Weeks of Strength Training

An interactive data story exploring how strength, fatigue, effort, and volume evolved across 13 exercises from January 22 to March 20, 2026.

REFERENCE — START HERE

Glossary

Unfamiliar with gym terminology? Click any term to expand its definition. You can also hover over highlighted terms throughout the visualization to see quick definitions inline.

Estimated 1RM Est. 1RM›
The maximum weight you could theoretically lift for a single repetition, calculated from your actual working sets using the Epley formula. Since testing your true 1RM is risky and exhausting, this estimate lets you track strength without maxing out every session.
1RM = weight × (1 + reps / 30)
RPE Rate of Perceived Exertion›
A 1–10 scale that measures how hard a set felt. An RPE of 10 means you gave absolutely everything and could not do another rep. RPE 7 means you had roughly 3 reps left in the tank. It's a way of tracking effort independent of the weight on the bar — the same weight can feel like RPE 7 one week and RPE 9 the next.
Set Volume ›
The total amount of work done in a single set, calculated as weight multiplied by repetitions. For example, lifting 185 lbs for 8 reps produces a set volume of 1,480 lbs. Summing set volume across all exercises gives you a measure of your total training workload for a session or week.
Set Volume = weight × reps
Within-Session Fatigue ›
The drop in performance between your first and second set of the same exercise within a single training session. In this dataset, fatigue is measured as the average rep drop from Set 1 to Set 2 across all sessions. A larger drop means the exercise causes more acute fatigue.
Compound Exercise ›
A multi-joint movement that recruits several large muscle groups simultaneously. Examples in this dataset include Bench Press, Hack Squat, Lat Pulldown, and Leg Press. Compounds are typically the heaviest lifts and form the foundation of a strength program, but they also tend to progress more slowly because they're already trained closer to their limit.
Accessory Exercise ›
A single-joint or isolation movement that targets a specific muscle. Examples in this dataset include Cable Chest Fly, Bicep Curl, Tricep Pushdown, and Machine Lateral Raise. Accessories are typically lighter and show faster percentage gains — partly because they start from a lower baseline and partly because of rapid early motor learning.
Trend Line (Linear Regression) ›
A straight line fitted through all data points for an exercise that represents the overall direction of progress. It's calculated using least-squares regression. Useful for cutting through session-to-session noise to see whether an exercise is genuinely trending up, flat, or down.
y = slope × session + intercept
Error Bar ›
A vertical line on a data point showing the range of values within that observation. In the small multiples chart, error bars show the spread between Set 1 and Set 2 estimated 1RM — the top is Set 1 (fresher), the bottom is Set 2 (fatigued).
Personal Record (PR) ›
A session where the estimated 1RM exceeded all previous sessions for that exercise. PRs are marked with a ⭐ gold star. A PR doesn't necessarily mean a new weight was used — it can result from getting more reps at the same weight, which produces a higher estimated 1RM.
Normalized % Change ›
A way of comparing exercises on the same scale regardless of actual weights. Each exercise's Week 1 value is treated as the baseline (0%), and all subsequent values show percentage change from that point. This lets you compare Cable Chest Fly at 70 lbs and Hack Squat at 247 lbs side by side fairly.
% change = (current − week1) / week1 × 100
Upper Body Split ›
A training session focused on muscles above the waist. Exercises: Bench Press, Cable Chest Fly, Incline Machine Chest Press, Lat Pulldown, Wide Grip Cable Row, Machine Lateral Raise, Straight Bar Bicep Curl, and Tricep Pushdown.
Lower Body Split ›
A training session focused on muscles below the waist. Exercises: Hack Squat, Leg Press, Leg Extension, Hamstring Curl, and Standing Calf Raise.
FEATURE 1 — ANIMATED REPLAY

Watch your training unfold in real time

Hit play to animate all 13 exercises session by session. Gold stars mark personal records. Use the filter to focus on one exercise, or watch all at once.

How to use: Hit play and watch exercises update in real time. Use the Focus dropdown to isolate one exercise. Gold ⭐ badges flash when a new personal record is set.
DESIGN CHALLENGE 1

How did each exercise progress over 9 weeks?

Full 9-week view with error bars and trend lines. Click any panel to link it to the fatigue explorer below. Double-click to enlarge. Panels sorted by % improvement.

Upper
Lower
--- Trend
⌶ S1→S2 error bar
⭐ PR
Key finding: Accessory exercises averaged significantly higher % gains than compounds over 9 weeks. Cable Chest Fly led at +48.6%, with Machine Lateral Raise close behind at +40.7%.

Set 1 vs Set 2 — Bench Press

Key finding: Bench Press and Bicep Curl show the steepest fatigue at 1.69 reps average drop. Every weight increase causes an immediate 1RM dip before recovering.
FEATURE 2 — WHAT-IF SIMULATOR

If your trend continues… what could you lift?

Project any exercise's estimated 1RM forward in time using your current trend line. Adjust the number of weeks to see different scenarios.

Exercise:
Weeks ahead: 4 weeks
—
Projection Chart
Note: Projections assume your linear trend continues unchanged. Real-world strength gains slow down over time — treat this as an optimistic upper bound, not a guarantee.
FEATURE 3 — EXERCISE COMPARISON

Compare any two exercises side by side

Select two exercises to overlay their normalized progression (% change from Week 1) on the same chart. Hover for exact values.

vs
Normalized Progression Comparison
Tip: Try comparing a compound lift like Bench Press with an accessory like Cable Chest Fly to see how differently they respond over the same 9 weeks.
DESIGN CHALLENGE 3

Did effort and volume move together?

Two separate charts for clarity. Weekly Volume shows total lbs lifted each week. Average RPE shows how hard those sessions felt on a 1–10 scale.

📦 Weekly Total Training Volume (lbs)
🔥 Weekly Average RPE (Rate of Perceived Exertion — 1 = easy, 10 = maximum effort)
Key finding: Volume stabilized around 42,600 lbs/week by Week 3. Despite similar volume, RPE climbed from 7.58 → 8.65 — same workload, progressively harder effort over 9 weeks.
DESIGN CHALLENGE 3 · HEATMAP

When were the gains actually happening?

% change from Week 1 per exercise per week. Click any cell to jump to that exercise's fatigue chart.

Key finding: Most gains concentrated in Weeks 2–4. By Weeks 6–9 progress clearly slowed across most exercises.
